policy_statement: Towards more sustainable food systems (2030 strategic objectives) covering agro-ecological production, Good Food availability, procurement/meal standards, and food waste reduction.
mechanism_description: The strategy aims to reduce food waste, which lowers emissions from producing, transporting, and disposing of wasted food and can reduce methane if less food ends up in landfill.
primary_causal_pathway: Food system demand reduction → ↓ food production + ↓ supply-chain energy use + ↓ organic waste to landfill → ↓ CO₂e (incl. ↓ CH₄) → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Households, retailers, institutions, and food service reduce edible food discards through prevention, redistribution, and improved inventory/portioning/planning.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological processes: Less food demanded and discarded reduces upstream agricultural emissions (fertilizer-related N₂O, enteric/land-use CO₂, on-farm energy), processing/cold-chain/transport energy (CO₂), and reduces anaerobic decomposition of organics in landfills (CH₄).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions (CO₂e), often dominated by avoided upstream production emissions, with additional landfill methane avoidance when diversion/prevention reduces landfilled organics.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding GHG emissions by preventing the production and supply-chain activities of food that would otherwise be wasted (with additional avoided landfill methane where applicable).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Reduced food waste lowers total food system throughput, yielding avoided lifecycle GHG emissions (upstream) and often reduced landfill CH₄—this is a direct emissions-reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it also improves resource efficiency (less input per delivered nutrition) and can reduce waste management burdens, the mechanism’s central climate-relevant outcome is lower CO₂e emissions, which maps best to Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency as the primary category.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy focuses only on diverting food waste to composting/AD without preventing waste, the dominant mechanism may shift toward “organic waste diversion → reduced landfill methane” (still Mitigation). If framed mainly as food security/social support, climate benefits may be secondary, but the shared mechanism as defined is emissions avoidance.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is “food waste reduction → upstream emissions + landfill methane reduction” inherently targets lower GHG emissions from avoided production and/or avoided landfill CH₄, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the dominant emissions savings come more from upstream agricultural avoidance vs. landfill methane avoidance (and whether redistribution vs. prevention is emphasized) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some “food waste” policies emphasize diversion (composting/anaerobic digestion) rather than prevention; those remain Mitigation but would be better represented by a different canonical mechanism. Additionally, if reduced waste leads to rebound consumption elsewhere, net upstream savings could be smaller; this does not change the primary category for the mechanism.
key_indicators: "2030 Strategic objectives"; "Towards more sustainable food systems"; "Relocating sustainable production"; "meets the demand for sustainable, accessible food"; "Reducing food waste"
co_benefits: Improved food security/accessibility; reduced household/municipal costs; reduced local pollution from waste; potential public health/nutrition improvements via “Good Food” availability.
instance_edge_case_notes: Objectives mention procurement/meal standards in the policy_statement, which could imply a future procurement requirement or performance standard, but the verbatim text provided only lists broad objectives without specifying any concrete instrument (targets, mandates, funding, or standards). Classified as planning-strategy based on the available text.
